sections.title: When to Use PrГӨteritum vs. Perfekt
sections.explanation: German has two main past tenses: Perfekt (conversational past) and PrГӨteritum (simple past/Imperfekt). In spoken German, Perfekt is the default past tense: 'Ich habe gegessen' (I ate). PrГӨteritum is preferred in written German вҖ” novels, newspapers, formal reports, and storytelling. However, the verbs 'sein', 'haben', and modal verbs (kГ¶nnen, mГјssen, etc.) almost always use PrГӨteritum even in speech. Nobody says 'Ich habe gehabt' or 'Ich bin gewesen' in normal conversation вҖ” instead: 'Ich hatte' and 'Ich war'. Learning PrГӨteritum is essential for reading German texts.
sections.tip: Rule of thumb: use Perfekt when speaking, PrГӨteritum when writing. But sein/haben/modals вҶ’ always PrГӨteritum, even in speech!

examples.en: I was tired yesterday. (PrГӨteritum вҖ” normal in speech)
He had no time. (PrГӨteritum вҖ” normal in speech)
She walked through the park. (PrГӨteritum in narrative)

examples.de: Sie ist durch den Park gegangen. (spoken)
examples.en: She walked through the park. (Perfekt in conversation)

exercises.prompt: Which past tense is preferred in everyday spoken German for most verbs?
options: Both are equally common in speech
exercises.explanation: In spoken German, Perfekt is the default past tense for most verbs. PrГӨteritum is mainly used in writing вҖ” except for sein, haben, and modal verbs.

exercises.prompt: Which verbs almost always use PrГӨteritum even in spoken German?
options: Action verbs like gehen, essen, spielen
All verbs use PrГӨteritum equally in speech
exercises.explanation: sein (war), haben (hatte), and modals (konnte, musste, etc.) are used in PrГӨteritum even in everyday conversation. It sounds unnatural to use Perfekt for these.

exercises.prompt: You are reading a German novel and see: 'Er Г¶ffnete die TГјr und ging hinein.' Why is PrГӨteritum used here?
options: Because it happened a long time ago
Because PrГӨteritum is the standard tense for written narratives
exercises.explanation: Written German narratives (novels, stories, news articles) use PrГӨteritum as their default past tense, regardless of when the event happened.

sections.title: Regular (Weak) Verbs
sections.explanation: Regular (weak) verbs form the PrГӨteritum by adding -te to the stem, then personal endings. Take the infinitive, remove -en to get the stem, then add: ich -te, du -test, er/sie/es -te, wir -ten, ihr -tet, sie/Sie -ten. Example: machen вҶ’ mach- вҶ’ ich machte, du machtest, er machte, wir machten, ihr machtet, sie machten. If the stem ends in -t or -d, add -ete: arbeiten вҶ’ ich arbeitete, du arbeitetest. Notice that ich and er/sie/es share the same form (no additional ending).
sections.tip: Pattern: stem + te/test/te/ten/tet/ten. The ich and er/sie/es forms are always identical in PrГӨteritum!

examples.de: machen вҶ’ ich machte, du machtest, er machte
examples.en: to do/make вҶ’ I did, you did, he did

examples.de: spielen вҶ’ sie spielte, wir spielten
examples.en: to play вҶ’ she played, we played

examples.de: wohnen вҶ’ ich wohnte in Berlin.
examples.en: to live вҶ’ I lived in Berlin.

examples.de: arbeiten вҶ’ er arbeitete den ganzen Tag.
examples.en: to work вҶ’ He worked the whole day.

exercises.prompt: Er ___ (machen) seine Hausaufgaben. вҖ” He did his homework.
exercises.explanation: machen вҶ’ mach + te = machte. The er/sie/es form takes -te with no additional ending.

exercises.prompt: What is the PrГӨteritum of 'spielen' for 'wir'?
exercises.explanation: wir takes -ten: spiel + ten = spielten.

exercises.prompt: Ich ___ (wohnen) frГјher in Hamburg. вҖ” I used to live in Hamburg.
exercises.explanation: wohnen вҶ’ wohn + te = wohnte. The ich form takes -te.

exercises.prompt: Match each pronoun to the correct PrГӨteritum form of 'machen':
exercises.explanation: Weak verb endings: ich -te, du -test, er/sie/es -te, wir -ten, ihr -tet, sie/Sie -ten.

sections.title: Irregular (Strong) Verbs
sections.explanation: Strong verbs change their stem vowel in the PrГӨteritum and do NOT add -te. Instead, the endings are: ich -(no ending), du -st, er/sie/es -(no ending), wir -en, ihr -t, sie/Sie -en. Again, ich and er/sie/es are identical. You must memorize the PrГӨteritum stem for each strong verb. Common ones: gehen вҶ’ ging, kommen вҶ’ kam, sehen вҶ’ sah, geben вҶ’ gab, nehmen вҶ’ nahm, sprechen вҶ’ sprach, fahren вҶ’ fuhr, schreiben вҶ’ schrieb, lesen вҶ’ las, finden вҶ’ fand, trinken вҶ’ trank, essen вҶ’ aГҹ, helfen вҶ’ half, stehen вҶ’ stand, liegen вҶ’ lag.
sections.tip: Strong verbs: vowel changes + zero ending for ich/er. Learn the PrГӨteritum stem вҖ” that's the ich/er form. Then add -st (du), -en (wir/sie), -t (ihr).

examples.de: gehen вҶ’ ich ging, du gingst, er ging, wir gingen
examples.en: to go вҶ’ I went, you went, he went, we went

examples.de: kommen вҶ’ sie kam, sie kamen
examples.en: to come вҶ’ she came, they came
He saw the accident.
She gave me the book.
We drove to Munich.
He wrote a letter.

exercises.prompt: Er ___ (gehen) nach Hause. вҖ” He went home.
exercises.explanation: gehen вҶ’ ging. Strong verb with vowel change eвҶ’i. No ending for er/sie/es.

exercises.prompt: What is the PrГӨteritum of 'kommen' for 'sie' (she)?
exercises.explanation: kommen вҶ’ kam. Strong verb: vowel change oвҶ’a, no -te ending. 'kommte' does not exist.

exercises.prompt: Wir ___ (sprechen) Гјber das Problem. вҖ” We spoke about the problem.
exercises.hint: sprechen вҶ’ sprach, then add -en for wir
exercises.explanation: sprechen вҶ’ sprach (stem). For wir: sprach + en = sprachen.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its PrГӨteritum (ich/er) form:
exercises.explanation: These are the most common strong verbs. The ich and er/sie/es forms are the bare PrГӨteritum stem with no ending.

exercises.prompt: Sie ___ (lesen) das Buch in zwei Tagen. вҖ” She read the book in two days.
exercises.explanation: lesen вҶ’ las. Strong verb with vowel change eвҶ’a.

sections.title: Mixed Verbs
sections.explanation: Mixed verbs combine features of both weak and strong verbs: they change their stem vowel (like strong verbs) AND add the -te suffix (like weak verbs). The most important mixed verbs are: kennen вҶ’ kannte (to know a person), bringen вҶ’ brachte (to bring), denken вҶ’ dachte (to think), wissen вҶ’ wusste (to know a fact), nennen вҶ’ nannte (to call/name), rennen вҶ’ rannte (to run), brennen вҶ’ brannte (to burn). The endings follow the weak pattern: -te, -test, -te, -ten, -tet, -ten.
sections.tip: Mixed verbs = vowel change + weak endings (-te). Think of 'bringen вҶ’ brachte': both the vowel changes AND it gets -te.

examples.de: kennen вҶ’ ich kannte, du kanntest
examples.en: to know (person) вҶ’ I knew, you knew
to bring вҶ’ he brought
to think вҶ’ she thought

examples.de: wissen вҶ’ ich wusste die Antwort.
examples.en: to know (fact) вҶ’ I knew the answer.

exercises.prompt: Ich ___ (kennen) ihn gut. вҖ” I knew him well.
exercises.explanation: kennen вҶ’ kannte. Mixed verb: eвҶ’a vowel change plus the weak -te ending.

exercises.prompt: What is the PrГӨteritum of 'denken' (to think)?
exercises.explanation: denken вҶ’ dachte. Mixed verb with a consonant change as well (nkвҶ’ch). Both the stem and the suffix change.

exercises.prompt: Sie ___ (bringen) Blumen mit. вҖ” She brought flowers along.
exercises.explanation: bringen вҶ’ brachte. The stem changes from 'bring' to 'brach', then adds -te.

sections.title: Modal Verbs in PrГӨteritum
sections.explanation: Modal verbs are extremely common in PrГӨteritum, even in spoken German. They follow the weak verb pattern (-te endings) but lose their umlaut. kГ¶nnen вҶ’ konnte (could), mГјssen вҶ’ musste (had to), dГјrfen вҶ’ durfte (was allowed to), wollen вҶ’ wollte (wanted to), sollen вҶ’ sollte (was supposed to), mГ¶gen вҶ’ mochte (liked). Conjugation follows the weak pattern: ich konnte, du konntest, er konnte, wir konnten, ihr konntet, sie konnten. Note: sollen and wollen have no umlaut to lose, so their change is minimal.
sections.tip: Modal PrГӨteritum = drop the umlaut + add -te endings. kГ¶nnenвҶ’konnte, mГјssenвҶ’musste, dГјrfenвҶ’durfte. sollen/wollen just add -te.

examples.en: I couldn't sleep.
He had to work.
She wasn't allowed to go out.
We wanted to go to the cinema.
He was supposed to come at 8 o'clock.
She didn't like coffee.

exercises.prompt: Ich ___ (kГ¶nnen) gestern nicht kommen. вҖ” I couldn't come yesterday.
exercises.hint: kГ¶nnen loses its umlaut вҶ’ konnte
exercises.explanation: kГ¶nnen вҶ’ konnte. Drop the umlaut (Г¶вҶ’o) and add -te.

exercises.prompt: Match each modal verb to its PrГӨteritum (ich) form:
exercises.explanation: kГ¶nnenвҶ’konnte, mГјssenвҶ’musste, dГјrfenвҶ’durfte, wollenвҶ’wollte, sollenвҶ’sollte, mГ¶genвҶ’mochte. All follow the weak -te pattern.

exercises.prompt: What is the PrГӨteritum of 'mГјssen' for 'wir'?
exercises.explanation: mГјssen вҶ’ musste (stem). For wir: musste + n = mussten. Note: 'mГјssten' is Konjunktiv II (would have to), not PrГӨteritum.

sections.title: sein, haben, werden in PrГӨteritum
sections.explanation: These three verbs are the most important PrГӨteritum forms to know, as they are used constantly вҖ” even in speech. sein вҶ’ war: ich war, du warst, er war, wir waren, ihr wart, sie waren. haben вҶ’ hatte: ich hatte, du hattest, er hatte, wir hatten, ihr hattet, sie hatten. werden вҶ’ wurde: ich wurde, du wurdest, er wurde, wir wurden, ihr wurdet, sie wurden. 'sein' is fully irregular. 'haben' follows a mixed pattern (hatt- + weak endings). 'werden' is a strong verb (vowel change eвҶ’u).
sections.tip: war/hatte/wurde вҖ” memorize these three by heart. You will encounter them on every page of German text!

examples.en: I was sick.
You were so nice.
We had a lot of fun.
He became a teacher.
It became dark.

exercises.prompt: Ich ___ (sein) gestern im BГјro. вҖ” I was at the office yesterday.
exercises.explanation: sein вҶ’ war. Completely irregular. ich war, du warst, er war.

exercises.prompt: Wir ___ (haben) keine Ahnung. вҖ” We had no idea.
exercises.hint: haben вҶ’ hatte, then add -n for wir
exercises.explanation: haben вҶ’ hatte (stem). For wir: hatte + n = hatten.

exercises.prompt: Match the pronoun to the correct PrГӨteritum of 'sein':
exercises.explanation: sein: ich war, du warst, er war, wir waren, ihr wart, sie/Sie waren.

exercises.prompt: What is the PrГӨteritum of 'werden' for 'sie' (she)?
exercises.explanation: werden вҶ’ wurde. Strong verb with vowel change. 'ward' is an archaic form sometimes seen in literature, but 'wurde' is the modern standard.
